nodes.text: She drove down in the small Toyota wagon that had been her grandfather's, the wagon she had been driving since 2046 when she had inherited it. The wagon had two hundred and eighty-something thousand miles on it. The wagon ran. Tem had been keeping it running through an arrangement with the mechanic in old-Waldport who had also kept her grandfather's wagon running before her.
She drove down through the rain. The Sitka stand. The alders. The cedars. The switchbacks. The highway. The connective stretch through the small wood.
She arrived at the Daily Grind at four-eighteen.
Lena was at the counter. Kai was at the corner table with his laptop open and his phone beside it. Finn was not yet there.

Tem came in. Lena, at the counter, registered her arrival the way Lena had been registering Tem's arrivals for eighteen months — the small flash of the gravity she had now named. She did not, this afternoon, look away from the registering.
Tem went to the corner table. She sat across from Kai. Kai showed her the pictures of the patch on his phone. Tem looked at them for a long time without speaking.
It means yeah, Kai. I'm not going to know what to say about it for a while. I have to sit with it.
Kai. I know. I'm — I'm tired. I have not slept properly in four days. I drove down because we said I would and because I knew the patches were going to be visible by today.
I have known the patches were coming for some time. I have not known what to do about them. I still don't. I'm asking you to give me a few days. I am not asking forever. I am asking a few days.
He sat back. He drank his coffee.
Lena, behind the counter, brought Tem's coffee over. She set it in front of her. She did not, in setting it down, say anything.
Lena said yeah. She went back to the counter.
The bell over the door rang at five-thirty-something.

Finn came in. He was in the wet thrift-store coat. He had the duffel over his shoulder. His face had on it the look of a man who had heard something on the drive that he had not yet figured out what to do with.
He came to the corner table. He sat in the fourth chair.
The radio in the truck turned itself on.

The radio. The one in the duffel. The shortwave from my grandfather. It turned itself on about twenty minutes into the drive down. I had not turned it on. I was driving. The hum came through the canvas and then a voice came through. The voice said one thing and then the radio cut out.
Tem went very still. Lena, behind the counter, registered Tem going still. Kai, at the table, registered it also.
You said a few days. We're not even at the end of the first day yet.
He was annoyed. Lena, watching from the counter, could see he was annoyed. He was not going to push it harder than he had pushed. He drank his coffee.
Tem. Are you — are you okay.
Yeah, Finn. I'm tired. The name landed harder than I was ready for it to land. I'm okay.
He set the duffel down on the floor beside the chair.
I'm going home. I'm going to sleep. The crow has been on the dashboard for six days. The crow needs to be at my apartment. I'll text in the morning.
He got up. He looked at the three of them at the table for a beat. He went to the door. The bell rang. He was gone.
I'm going home too. I'm not — I'm not mad. I just don't have anything to do here. The patch is on the wall. The wall isn't going anywhere. I'll text in the morning.
Kai got up. He pulled on his coat. He walked to the counter.
He stood at the counter for a beat. Lena registered that he was about to say something else and then decided not to.
Tem and Lena were the only two people in the Daily Grind. Soren was in the back. The bell had rung three times in five minutes. The shop was empty.
Do you want to come over.

Not — Tem. Not for that. I have soup. I have leftover bread. I have a couch. You haven't slept properly in four days. The cabin is forty minutes up the road. My apartment is a block. Come over. Eat soup. Sleep on the couch. Drive up tomorrow.
She went to the back to tell Soren she was closing.
Outside the Daily Grind the rain was steady. The light was the slate-gray of a late afternoon in late August on the Oregon coast.
Across town the patch on the sanderling mural was where it was. In Lena's alley the patch on the Starfish Nebula was where it was.
The piece of charred wood was in Finn's duffel in his truck on the way to his apartment. The crow was on the dashboard.
Tem sat at the corner table. Lena came back from the back with her bag and her coat. The two of them walked the four blocks to Lena's apartment in the rain.
They did not, on the walk, say much.
